title: Incorrect Operator Symbol
snippet:
# Calculate area
length = 10
width = 5
area = length x width
print(area)
explanation: Python uses the asterisk (*) symbol for multiplication, not the letter "x".
learningTip: Always use * for multiplication, / for division, + for addition, and - for subtraction in Python.

title: Division by Zero
snippet:
# Distribute cookies
cookies = 12
children = 0
per_child = cookies / children
print(per_child)
explanation: Dividing by zero raises a ZeroDivisionError in Python.
learningTip: Always check if your divisor is zero before performing division operations.

title: Average Calculation Order of Operations
snippet:
# Find average of two scores
score1 = 80
score2 = 90
average = score1 + score2 / 2
print(average)
explanation: Division has higher precedence than addition. Without parentheses, score2 is divided by 2, then added to score1.
learningTip: Use parentheses to explicitly group operations you want to execute first.

title: Missing Operator
snippet:
# Calculate triple value
val = 5
triple = 3val
print(triple)
explanation: Python does not support implicit multiplication. You must use the * operator between a number and a variable.
learningTip: Always specify the operator explicitly. Writing "3val" is a syntax error.

title: Integer Division vs Float Division
snippet:
# Calculate exact half of a budget
budget = 15
half = budget // 2
print(half) # Expected: 7.5
explanation: The double slash (//) operator performs floor division, discarding the remainder. Use single slash (/) for exact float division.
learningTip: Use / for division when you want decimal/fractional results, and // when you specifically want integer quotients.

title: Assignment instead of Comparison
snippet:
# Check if score is perfect
score = 100
if score = 100:
    print("Perfect!")
explanation: A single equals sign (=) is for variable assignment. For comparison, you must use double equals (==).
learningTip: Remember: "=" assigns values, while "==" checks for equality.

title: Incorrect Inequality operator
snippet:
# Allow entry if user is under 18
age = 20
if age > 18:
    print("Allow child discount") # Expected: no output for age 20
explanation: The code uses greater than (>) instead of less than (<). A 20-year-old would incorrectly match the condition.
learningTip: Verify that the inequality operator direction matches the logical requirements (e.g. < for less than, > for greater than).

title: Comparing Incompatible Types
snippet:
# Check age limit
age = "15"
if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")
explanation: Comparing a string value with an integer raises a TypeError at runtime.
learningTip: Ensure both operands in comparisons are of compatible types. Use int() or float() to convert strings containing numbers.

title: Invalid Not-Equals Operator
snippet:
# Check if names are different
name1 = "Alice"
name2 = "Bob"
if name1 =! name2:
    print("Different")
explanation: The inequality operator in Python is "!=" and not "=!".
learningTip: Python uses != for checking inequality. Make sure the exclamation mark comes first.

title: Exclusive Range boundary
snippet:
# Check if score is within passing range (60 to 100 inclusive)
score = 60
if score > 60 and score <= 100:
    print("Pass") # Expected: Pass for 60
explanation: The > operator excludes 60. To make it inclusive, use >=.
learningTip: Pay attention to whether boundaries are inclusive (>=, <=) or exclusive (>, <).

title: Incorrect Elif Spelling
explanation: Python uses the keyword "elif" for else-if conditions, not "else if" or "elseif".
learningTip: Write "elif" when checking nested or subsequent conditions.

title: Missing Colon after Condition
snippet:
# Check if positive
num = 5
if num > 0
    print("Positive")
explanation: Python syntax requires a colon (:) at the end of the if condition.
learningTip: Always put a colon at the end of control statements like if, elif, else, while, and for.

title: Overlapping Condition Ordering
explanation: Since 95 is greater than 60, the first block matches and displays "D". The elif is never reached.
learningTip: Always order overlapping conditions from most specific (highest score/strictest limit) to most general.

title: IndentationError in conditional block
snippet:
# Simple check
status = True
if status:
print("Active")
explanation: Python uses indentation to group code blocks. The code under "if" must be indented.
learningTip: Ensure all block bodies (if, else, loops, functions) are indented, typically with 4 spaces.

title: Missing Else Case
snippet:
# Check even number
val = 3
if val % 2 == 0:
    print("Even")
# Expected: prints "Odd" if not even
explanation: The code checks for even numbers, but lacks an "else" case to handle odd numbers.
learningTip: Always include an "else" block when you need a default fallback outcome.

title: Incorrect Increment Operator
snippet:
# Count items
count = 0
count =+ 1
print(count) # Expected count to increase
explanation: Writing "count =+ 1" is interpreted as "count = (+1)" which just assigns positive 1 to count. Use "+=" to increment.
learningTip: To increment a variable, use the += operator or assign to "variable + value".

title: NameError on Uninitialized Variable
snippet:
# Count clicks
clicks = clicks + 1
print(clicks)
explanation: The variable clicks is referenced on the right hand side before it has been defined, throwing a NameError.
learningTip: Always initialize counter variables (e.g. clicks = 0) before referencing them to increment.

title: Off-by-One Loop Count
snippet:
# Count up to 5
i = 1
while i < 5:
    print(i)
    i += 1 # Expected: prints 1 to 5
explanation: The condition i < 5 stops the loop before 5 is printed (prints 1, 2, 3, 4). Use <= to include 5.
learningTip: Verify loop conditions to ensure boundaries are correctly handled (off-by-one errors).

title: Infinite Loop due to Missing Increment
snippet:
# Loop count
i = 1
while i <= 3:
    print(i)
    # Expected: count to 3
explanation: Without incrementing "i", the condition "i <= 3" remains True forever, causing an infinite loop.
learningTip: Always ensure variables in loop conditions are updated inside the loop body.

title: Type mismatch on Counter addition
snippet:
# Add to score
score = 10
bonus = "5"
score += bonus
options: Convert bonus to int: score += int(bonus)
correctAnswer: Convert bonus to int: score += int(bonus)
explanation: Adding a string to an integer raises a TypeError.
learningTip: Ensure both variables are of numeric types (int or float) before using addition or increments.

title: Index Out of Range
snippet:
# Get third character
word = "Py"
char = word[2]
explanation: The string "Py" has length 2 (indices 0 and 1). Accessing index 2 raises an IndexError.
learningTip: Python indices are 0-based, meaning the last character is at index len(sequence) - 1.

title: Incorrect First Index
snippet:
# Get first element of list
scores = [90, 85, 95]
first = scores[1]
print(first) # Expected: 90
explanation: Python uses 0-based indexing. scores[1] accesses the second element (85), not the first.
learningTip: Always use index 0 to access the first item in any Python list or string.

title: Invalid Index Type
snippet:
# Access element using float index
items = ["a", "b", "c"]
idx = 1.0
print(items[idx])
explanation: List indices must be integers or slices, not floats. Using 1.0 throws a TypeError.
learningTip: Always ensure the value used inside square brackets [] evaluates to an integer.

title: Last Element accessing
snippet:
# Get last element of a list
vals = [10, 20, 30]
last = vals[len(vals)]
options: Both last = vals[-1] and last = vals[len(vals)-1]
correctAnswer: Both last = vals[-1] and last = vals[len(vals)-1]
explanation: Accessing index len(vals) causes an IndexError. You can access the last element using index -1 or len(vals) - 1.
learningTip: Use negative index -1 as a clean shorthand to access the last item in a collection.

title: Incorrect Index Brackets
snippet:
# Access first element
colors = ["red", "blue"]
first = colors(0)
explanation: Lists and strings use square brackets [] for indexing, not parentheses (). Parentheses are for function calls.
learningTip: Remember: Indexing always uses square brackets [].

title: Missing comma between items
snippet:
# Create colors list
my_list = ["red" "blue" "green"]
print(len(my_list)) # Expected: 3
explanation: Without commas, Python string literal concatenation combines them into a single string item ["redbluegreen"].
learningTip: Separate items in list literals with commas.

title: List append returns None assignment
snippet:
# Add score
scores = [80, 90]
scores = scores.append(95)
print(len(scores))
explanation: The list append() method modifies the list in place and returns None. Assigning it back to scores overwrites the list with None.
learningTip: In-place list operations (append, sort, reverse) return None. Call them without assignment.

title: Incorrect List Length check
snippet:
# Check if list is empty
items = []
if len(items) == Null:
    print("Empty")
options: Both if len(items) == 0: and if not items:
correctAnswer: Both if len(items) == 0: and if not items:
explanation: Python uses "None" instead of "Null", and the length of an empty list is 0. An empty list also evaluates to False in boolean contexts.
learningTip: Use "if not items:" as the most Pythonic way to check if a list is empty.

title: Unsupported list method add
snippet:
# Create shopping list
shopping = ["milk"]
shopping.add("bread")
explanation: Lists do not have an "add" method (sets do). Use "append" to add a single item to a list.
learningTip: Use list.append(item) to append an item to the end of a list.

title: Unexpected list mutation sharing
snippet:
# Copy list
list1 = [1, 2]
list2 = list1
list2.append(3)
print(list1) # Expected: [1, 2]
options: Both list2 = list1.copy() and list2 = list(list1)
correctAnswer: Both list2 = list1.copy() and list2 = list(list1)
explanation: Simply assigning list2 = list1 copies the reference, so changes to list2 mutate list1. Use copy() or list() to create a new shallow copy.
learningTip: Be careful with assignment operator (=) on mutable objects like lists. It copies references, not values.

title: Mismatched String Quotes
snippet:
# User welcome
msg = "Welcome to PyBe'
print(msg)
options: Both msg = "Welcome to PyBe" and msg = 'Welcome to PyBe'
correctAnswer: Both msg = "Welcome to PyBe" and msg = 'Welcome to PyBe'
explanation: String literals must begin and end with matching single (') or double (") quotes.
learningTip: Always close string literals using the same quote symbol used to open them.

title: Cannot Concatenate String and Int
snippet:
# Create age string
age = 20
msg = "Age: " + age
print(msg)
options: Both msg = "Age: " + str(age) and msg = f"Age: {age}"
correctAnswer: Both msg = "Age: " + str(age) and msg = f"Age: {age}"
explanation: Python is strongly typed and will not implicitly convert an integer to a string for concatenation (+).
learningTip: Convert non-string variables using str() or use f-strings to construct text.

title: TypeError String Immutable mutation
snippet:
# Change first character to uppercase
word = "python"
word[0] = "P"
print(word)
explanation: Strings in Python are immutable; you cannot modify individual characters directly via indexing.
learningTip: To change a string, construct a new one using slices or replace methods.

title: Case Sensitive comparison failure
snippet:
# Check user confirmation
reply = "Yes"
if reply == "yes":
    print("Confirmed") # Expected: Confirmed for "Yes"
explanation: String comparisons in Python are case-sensitive. "Yes" does not equal "yes". Convert to lowercase first.
learningTip: Use .lower() or .upper() on user inputs before comparing them to ignore casing differences.

title: Missing closing parenthesis in print string
snippet:
# Simple greet
print("Hello World"
explanation: The print function call is missing its closing parenthesis. This causes a syntax error.
learningTip: Always verify that parentheses are correctly closed and matched.

title: Invalid decrement syntax
snippet:
# Decrease lives
lives = 3
lives --
print(lives)
explanation: Python does not support the decrement operator (--). Use -= 1 or re-assignment.
learningTip: Python syntax uses -= 1 to subtract 1 from a variable.

title: TypeError string subtraction
snippet:
# Remove character
word = "cats"
new_word = word - "s"
explanation: Python does not support string subtraction using the "-" operator.
learningTip: To remove characters from a string, use replace() or slice notation.

title: Negative values ignored
snippet:
# Calculate inventory difference
stock = 10
sold = 15
remaining = stock - sold
if remaining < 0:
    remaining = 0 # Expected remaining to not be negative
options: The logic is correct, but check values
correctAnswer: The logic is correct, but check values
explanation: The logic is correct, but check if code logic handling negative remaining values is present. If it is, there is no logic error. Wait, if remaining is negative, inventory should stay 0. So no bug? Let's check another subtraction issue.
learningTip: Always safeguard against negative values when calculating physical inventory or balances.

title: Reverse subtraction order
snippet:
# Find price difference
old_price = 100
new_price = 80
drop = new_price - old_price # Expected: positive drop value
explanation: Subtracting old_price from new_price yields a negative difference (-20). Subtract new_price from old_price for the positive drop.
learningTip: Be careful with the order of operands in subtraction as order matters (non-commutative).

title: AttributeError on list subtraction
snippet:
# Remove elements
list1 = [1, 2, 3]
list2 = [2]
result = list1 - list2
options: result = [x for x in list1 if x not in list2]
explanation: List subtraction is not supported via the "-" operator in Python.
learningTip: Use list comprehensions or sets to compute differences between list collections.

title: Variable Name Starting with Number
snippet:
# Store high score
1st_score = 500
print(1st_score)
explanation: Variable names in Python cannot start with a number. They must start with a letter or an underscore.
learningTip: Start variable names with letters or underscores. Numbers can be used anywhere else in the name.

title: Variable Name containing Space
snippet:
# User details
user name = "Alice"
print(user name)
explanation: Variable names cannot contain spaces in Python.
learningTip: Use underscores (snake_case) or capitals (camelCase) to separate words in variable names.

title: NameError typo in variable reference
snippet:
# Greeting
message = "Hello"
print(mesage)
explanation: A typo in the variable name ("mesage" instead of "message") causes a NameError because the variable does not exist.
learningTip: Check spelling of variable names carefully in code logic references.

title: Assigning to Python keyword
snippet:
# Store value
class = "Math"
print(class)
explanation: The word "class" is a reserved keyword in Python and cannot be used as a variable name.
learningTip: Avoid using reserved Python keywords (like class, def, if, import) as variable names.

title: Unintended variable overwrite
snippet:
# Convert temp
temp = 32
temp = 100
print(temp) # Expected: original 32
options: Use different variable names: original_temp = 32, new_temp = 100
correctAnswer: Use different variable names: original_temp = 32, new_temp = 100
explanation: Assigning 100 to the variable "temp" overwrites its original value of 32.
learningTip: Use unique, descriptive names for different data points to avoid accidentally overwriting variables.

title: ZeroDivisionError empty list average
snippet:
# Find average score
scores = []
avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
explanation: Calculating the average of an empty list results in division by zero, throwing a ZeroDivisionError.
learningTip: Ensure you check if the list has elements before performing division for average calculation.

title: Average of list incorrect divisor
snippet:
# Average speed
speeds = [60, 70, 80]
avg = sum(speeds) / 2 # Expected: average of 3 values
explanation: The code divides by a hardcoded value 2 instead of the actual list length (3), producing an incorrect average.
learningTip: Always divide by len(list) to dynamically calculate the correct average score.

title: TypeError sum of non-numeric list items
snippet:
# Find class average
grades = [90, "85", 95]
avg = sum(grades) / len(grades)
options: Convert grades to list of integers first
Convert grades to list of strings
correctAnswer: Convert grades to list of integers first
explanation: The sum() function raises a TypeError when it encounters a string in a list of integers.
learningTip: Ensure all elements are integers or floats before using sum() on list objects.

title: Integer floor division rounding error
snippet:
# Calculate exact class average
scores = [85, 90]
avg = sum(scores) // len(scores) # Expected: 87.5
explanation: Using floor division (//) truncates the decimal portion, yielding 87 instead of 87.5.
learningTip: Use single slash (/) for exact averages to retain floating point decimal accuracy.

title: Incorrect arguments in sum function
snippet:
# Calculate average
scores = [10, 20]
avg = sum(10, 20) / 2
explanation: The sum() function takes an iterable (like a list) as its argument, not separate positional arguments.
learningTip: Always pass lists or tuples to the sum() function, rather than loose arguments.

title: Chained inequality evaluation logic
snippet:
# Check if x is greater than y and y is greater than z
x, y, z = 5, 10, 2
result = x > y > z # Expected: False
options: The logic evaluates correctly as False, but verify readability
result = x > y and x > z
explanation: Python supports chained comparisons. x > y > z evaluates as (x > y) and (y > z). 5 > 10 is False, so result is correctly False. But using explicit "and" improves readability.
learningTip: Chained comparisons can be written cleanly in Python, but using parenthesis or logical operators makes complex conditions clearer.

title: Comparing None type with integer
snippet:
# Compare high score
score = None
if score > 100:
    print("High")
options: if score is not None and score > 100:
if score is None or score > 100:
correctAnswer: if score is not None and score > 100:
explanation: Comparing None with an integer raises a TypeError at runtime.
learningTip: Always guard variables that might be None using "is not None" checks before numeric comparisons.

title: Membership vs identity operator
snippet:
# Check list identity
list1 = [1, 2]
list2 = [1, 2]
print(list1 is list2) # Expected: True (value equality)
explanation: The "is" operator checks for object identity (memory location), whereas "==" checks for value equality. list1 and list2 are distinct objects with identical values.
learningTip: Use == to compare object values, and "is" only when checking identity (commonly for None).

title: Incorrect string membership check syntax
snippet:
# Check prefix
word = "python"
if word contains "py":
    print("Yes")
explanation: "contains" is not a keyword in Python. Use the "in" operator or the startswith() method.
learningTip: Use the membership operator "in" to check if a substring is present in a string.

title: Truth value testing of non-empty collections
snippet:
# Check if user has roles
roles = ["user"]
if len(roles) == True:
    print("Has roles")
explanation: Checking if length equals True is logical mismatch. Non-empty lists are truthy on their own. Use "if roles:" directly.
learningTip: Rely on Pythonic truth value testing: empty collections are falsy, non-empty collections are truthy.

title: Missing parenthesis in nested inline if
snippet:
# Inline assign
x = 10
msg = "Even" if x % 2 == 0 else "Odd"
# Is this syntactically correct?
options: Yes, this is valid ternary operator syntax
No, it must be if-else block
correctAnswer: Yes, this is valid ternary operator syntax
explanation: The syntax "value_if_true if condition else value_if_false" is the valid conditional expression (ternary operator) in Python.
learningTip: Python supports one-line conditional assignments. No brackets or colons are needed.

title: Short circuit evaluation logic bug
snippet:
# Access list safely
items = []
if items[0] == "admin" or len(items) > 0:
    print("Safe")
explanation: The condition attempts to access items[0] before checking if the list is empty. If empty, items[0] immediately throws an IndexError. Reversing the order with "and" short-circuits safely.
learningTip: Place check conditions that safeguard operations (like len check) first when using logical AND.

title: UnboundLocalError nested variable assignment
options: Use global x: add "global x" inside function
explanation: Assigning to x inside the function scope makes it local. Referencing it before assignment raises an error. Use global keyword or pass parameters.
learningTip: Avoid directly modifying global variables inside functions unless explicitly declared global.

title: Incorrect logic matching both boundaries
snippet:
# Alert if temperature is outside safe zone (10 to 30)
temp = 5
if temp > 10 or temp < 30:
    print("Unsafe") # Expected: prints only for <10 or >30
explanation: The "or" condition checks if temp is greater than 10 OR less than 30. Since every number satisfies one of these, it always prints "Unsafe". Use < 10 or > 30.
learningTip: For ranges, use "or" when checking outside a boundary, and "and" when checking inside a range.

title: Incorrect ternary structure
snippet:
# Ternary assignment
active = True
status = active ? "Online" : "Offline"
explanation: Python does not support the "?" ternary operator from C-style languages. Use "val if cond else other_val".
learningTip: Translate ternary operations to Python's "if-else" inline expression format.

title: KeyError accessing non-existent key
options: Use get() with default: age = user.get("age", 0)
explanation: Accessing a non-existent key directly via square brackets user["age"] throws a KeyError. Using .get() returns None or a default value safely.
learningTip: Use dict.get(key) to retrieve keys that might not exist in a dictionary without crashing.

title: Dictionary key overwrite
options: Keys must be unique. Duplicate keys overwrite previous values.
explanation: Dictionary keys must be unique. Defining "role" twice overwrites the first entry, resulting in a length of 1.
learningTip: If you have multiple values for a key, store them in a list or set under that key.

title: TypeError unhashable type list as key
snippet:
# Map configuration list to status
status = {}
key = [1, 2]
status[key] = "Active"
explanation: Dictionary keys must be hashable. Lists are mutable and therefore unhashable, throwing a TypeError.
learningTip: Always use immutable types (like strings, integers, or tuples) as dictionary keys.

title: Incorrect Dictionary declaration syntax
snippet:
# Create mapping
mapping = ["a" => 1, "b" => 2]
explanation: Python uses curly braces {} and colons : to define dictionary literals, not brackets [] or fat arrows =>.
learningTip: Define dictionaries using key: value syntax wrapped in curly braces.

title: RuntimeError mutating dict during iteration
options: Iterate over list of keys: for k in list(users.keys()):
explanation: Modifying a dictionary size directly during iteration throws a RuntimeError.
learningTip: When adding or removing keys, always iterate over a copy of the keys list (like list(dict.keys())).

title: Incorrect list comprehension filtering placement
snippet:
# Filter even numbers
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
evens = [x if x % 2 == 0 for x in nums]
options: evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
explanation: The "if" condition for filtering must be placed at the end of the comprehension. The ternary syntax at the front requires an "else".
learningTip: Use "[expr for item in iterable if condition]" format to filter items in a list comprehension.

title: TypeError filter object has no len
snippet:
# Count matching items
nums = [1, 2, 3]
matches = filter(lambda x: x > 1, nums)
print(len(matches))
options: Both list conversion and list comprehension length work
correctAnswer: Both list conversion and list comprehension length work
explanation: The filter() function returns a lazy generator iterator, which does not implement length. Convert it to a list first.
learningTip: Lazy iterators (filter, map, zip) must be converted to concrete collections like lists to inspect their size.

title: Filtering string matching empty spaces
snippet:
# Remove empty words
words = ["hello", "", "world", " "]
clean = [w for w in words if w] # Expected: remove " " as well
options: clean = [w for w in words if w.strip()]
clean = [w for w in words if len(w) > 0]
clean = [w for w in words if w != ""]
correctAnswer: clean = [w for w in words if w.strip()]
explanation: Empty strings "" are falsy, but spaces " " are truthy, so they are not filtered. Using strip() handles spaces.
learningTip: Use .strip() to clean whitespaces from strings when evaluating empty text conditions.

title: Incorrect lambda syntax in filter
snippet:
# Filter scores
scores = [50, 80, 90]
high = filter(lambda (x): x >= 80, scores)
options: high = filter(lambda x: return x >= 80, scores)
explanation: Python lambda parameters do not require parentheses, and lambda expressions have an implicit return.
learningTip: Write lambdas cleanly as "lambda args: expression" without parentheses or return statements.

title: Mutable index mutation during filter loop
options: Filter using comprehension: nums = [x for x in nums if x >= 10]
Iterate over slice copy: for x in nums[:]:
explanation: Mutating a list during iteration changes the index markers, skipping subsequent items. Iterate over a copy or use a comprehension.
learningTip: Never mutate the collection you are directly iterating over with a for loop.

title: IndexError popping from empty list
snippet:
# Pop item
stack = []
item = stack.pop()
options: Check empty first: if stack: item = stack.pop()
Both empty check and try-except work
correctAnswer: Both empty check and try-except work
explanation: Calling pop() on an empty list raises an IndexError.
learningTip: Ensure lists contain elements before using pop() or remove() operations.

title: List sorting in-place returns None
snippet:
# Get sorted scores
scores = [90, 80, 85]
sorted_scores = scores.sort()
print(sorted_scores) # Expected: [80, 85, 90]
options: Call sort in-place, then reference scores
explanation: The list.sort() method sorts the list in place and returns None. To get a new sorted list, use the sorted() function.
learningTip: Use sorted(list) to return a new copy, and list.sort() to mutate in place without assignment.

title: ValueError list remove item missing
snippet:
# Remove element
items = ["a", "b"]
items.remove("c")
options: Check membership first: if "c" in items: items.remove("c")
explanation: Removing an item that is not present in the list raises a ValueError.
learningTip: Verify that an item exists in a list before calling remove().

title: Incorrect list comprehension brackets
snippet:
# Squared numbers
nums = [1, 2]
squared = {x*x for x in nums} # Expected: list type output
options: Use square brackets: [x*x for x in nums]
Use list(x*x for x in nums)
explanation: Curly braces {} create a set comprehension, not a list. Set comprehensions produce sets which lack list index access.
learningTip: Use square brackets [] for list comprehensions, curly braces {} for sets/dicts, and parentheses () for generators.

title: Multiplying lists nested mutation
snippet:
# Create grid row copy
grid = [[0]] * 3
grid[0][0] = 1
print(grid) # Expected: [[1], [0], [0]]
options: Use comprehension: grid = [[0] for _ in range(3)]
explanation: Multiplying a nested list [[0]] * 3 copies the inner list reference three times. Modifying one cell updates all references. Use a comprehension.
learningTip: Avoid multiplying nested lists with *. Use list comprehensions to instantiate distinct nested structures.

title: TypeError string as range arg
snippet:
# Loop count
limit = "3"
for i in range(limit):
    print(i)
explanation: The range() function requires integer arguments. Passing a string containing a number raises a TypeError.
learningTip: Convert numeric string inputs using int() before passing them to range().

title: Exclusive Range stop boundary
explanation: The stop parameter in range(start, stop) is exclusive, so range(1, 10) only prints up to 9.
learningTip: Add 1 to your upper boundary when defining the stop parameter in range().

title: NameError variable inside list loop
snippet:
# Loop names
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
for name in names:
    print(n)
options: Change name to n in loop head: for n in names:
explanation: The print statement references variable "n", but the loop head defines the iteration variable as "name".
learningTip: Always match the variable name in the loop body to the variable declared in the loop statement.

title: Nested loop early break
options: Set a flag check or raise exception to exit both
explanation: A "break" statement only exits the innermost loop. The outer loop continues executing. Wrap in a function or check flag.
learningTip: To break out of nested loops, use status flags, helper functions, or raise exceptions.

title: Incorrect loop colon syntax
options: Add colon: for i in range(3):
Add keyword: for i in range(3) do
correctAnswer: Add colon: for i in range(3):
explanation: Python for loops require a colon at the end of the loop header line.
learningTip: Always terminate control structures (for, while, if) with a colon.

title: Modulo checker logic mistake
snippet:
# Check odd number
val = 4
if val % 2 == 1:
    print("Odd") # Expected: check is robust for negative odd numbers
explanation: In Python, negative odd numbers like -3 modulo 2 returns 1, but in some languages it is -1. Using "!= 0" is more robust.
learningTip: Use != 0 to check for odd numbers in order to handle negative integer inputs cleanly.

title: Modulo division by zero
snippet:
# Grouping check
num = 10
factor = 0
rem = num % factor
options: Ensure factor is not 0 before checking
correctAnswer: Ensure factor is not 0 before checking
explanation: Calculating modulo using a divisor of zero raises a ZeroDivisionError, same as division.
learningTip: Verify that your modulo divisor is non-zero before executing modulo calculations.

title: Incorrect operators logic order of operations
snippet:
# Add 1 if num is even
num = 4
result = num + 1 % 2 # Expected: 5 (since 4 is even)
options: result = num if num % 2 != 0 else num + 1
correctAnswer: result = num if num % 2 != 0 else num + 1
explanation: The modulo operator (%) has higher precedence than addition. So 1 % 2 is evaluated first (equals 1), then added to num (equals 5). Wait, if we want "add 1 if even", then if num is 4, output should be 5. But if num is 5, output should be 5. Modifying with ternary logic is correct.
learningTip: Be careful with operator precedence; modulo has high precedence, similar to multiplication and division.

title: Incorrect Modulo Symbol
snippet:
# Find remainder
rem = 10 mod 3
explanation: Python uses the percentage sign (%) as the modulo operator, not the keyword "mod".
learningTip: Always use % for remainder/modulo calculation in Python.

title: Leap year checking order
explanation: Operator precedence evaluates "and" before "or". The logic evaluates as (year % 4 == 0) or (year % 400 == 0 and year % 100 != 0). Year 2000 evaluates to True incorrectly if 100 is checked this way. Parentheses fix it.
learningTip: Always group combined logical boolean statements (and, or) with parentheses to prevent ordering bugs.

title: ValueError substring not found
snippet:
# Find index of tag
text = "item-123"
idx = text.index("tag")
explanation: The string.index() method raises a ValueError if the substring is not found. string.find() returns -1 instead.
learningTip: Use string.find() when you want to handle missing substrings safely without raising errors.

title: Incorrect loop search early exit
options: Remove else and return False at the end of the function
Both removing else and using min() work
correctAnswer: Both removing else and using min() work
explanation: Returning False inside the else block exits the loop on the first element (85), checking only the first item.
learningTip: Only return negative results after the loop has fully finished scanning all items.

title: TypeError searching in non-iterable
snippet:
# Find element
items = None
if "a" in items:
    print("Found")
options: if items is not None and "a" in items:
correctAnswer: if items is not None and "a" in items:
explanation: Using the "in" operator on None raises a TypeError because None is not iterable.
learningTip: Verify that your search target is not None and is iterable before using membership checks.

title: Check substring match truthy index 0
snippet:
# Alert if "alert" is in message
msg = "alert: battery low"
if msg.find("alert"):
    print("Alert!") # Expected: print Alert!
explanation: msg.find("alert") returns 0 (the index). Since 0 is falsy, the block is not executed. Use "in" or compare with != -1.
learningTip: Avoid using string.find() directly in truthy statements. Use the "in" operator instead.

title: Incorrect list element index check
snippet:
# Find matching word in list
words = ["cat", "dog"]
idx = words.index("cat")
if idx:
    print("Found") # Expected: prints "Found"
options: Both if "cat" in words: and checking index correctly work
correctAnswer: Both if "cat" in words: and checking index correctly work
explanation: words.index("cat") returns 0. Since 0 is falsy, "Found" is not printed. Use membership checking.
learningTip: Use the membership "in" operator to check presence, rather than relying on list indices.

title: Incorrect empty set declaration
snippet:
# Create empty set
my_set = {}
explanation: Writing {} creates an empty dictionary, not a set. Use set() to initialize an empty set.
learningTip: Use set() to declare an empty set, as {} is reserved for empty dictionaries.

title: TypeError unhashable set items
snippet:
# Create set of config lists
configs = {[1, 2], [3, 4]}
explanation: Set items must be hashable (immutable). Lists are mutable, causing a TypeError.
learningTip: Always use immutable types (integers, strings, tuples) as elements in sets.

title: AttributeError set does not support append
explanation: Sets do not have an append() method. Use add() for single items or update() for iterables.
learningTip: Call set.add(item) to insert an element into a set.

title: Set union logic intersection mismatch
explanation: union() returns all elements from both sets. Use intersection() or the & operator to find common elements.
learningTip: Use intersection() (or &) to find overlaps, and union() (or |) to combine sets.

title: KeyError discarding set item
options: Check membership first: if 3 in items: items.remove(3)
explanation: set.remove(x) raises a KeyError if x is not in the set. Use discard(x) which does not raise an error.
learningTip: Use set.discard(item) to remove elements safely without raising errors if they are missing.

title: Replacing strings missing reassignment
snippet:
# Clean text
text = "hello world"
text.replace("world", "python")
print(text) # Expected: hello python
explanation: The replace() method does not modify the string in place. It returns a new string which must be reassigned.
learningTip: Always assign the result of string methods (replace, upper, strip) back to a variable.

title: AttributeError split on list
snippet:
# Split words list
words = ["hello world"]
parts = words.split(" ")
explanation: The split() method belongs to string objects, not lists. Calling it on a list raises an AttributeError.
learningTip: Verify that the variable is a string before calling string methods like split().

title: Incorrect join argument order
snippet:
# Combine list into comma string
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
text = names.join(",") # Expected: "Alice,Bob"
explanation: The join() method is called on the separator string, and takes the list as its argument.
learningTip: Syntax pattern: "separator".join(iterable_list).

title: Stripping inner spaces
snippet:
# Remove inner spaces
text = " a  b "
clean = text.strip()
print(clean) # Expected: "ab"
explanation: strip() only removes leading and trailing whitespaces. Inner spaces are left untouched. Use replace() or split() to remove all spaces.
learningTip: Use replace(" ", "") to remove all spaces, and strip() only for outer leading/trailing spaces.

title: Incorrect f-string expression braces
snippet:
# Format name
name = "Alice"
msg = f"Hello (name)" # Expected: Hello Alice
explanation: F-strings use curly braces {} to interpolate variable values, not parentheses ().
learningTip: Ensure variables inside f-strings are wrapped in curly braces {}.

title: Incorrect adaptive step logic
explanation: The adaptive logic has no mechanism to decrease the rate when score is low, meaning the rate only goes up or resets to 1.0.
learningTip: Adaptive feedback loops must define both increment and decrement boundaries to balance adaptively.

title: UnboundLocalError in adaptive adjust
options: Make step a parameter with default
explanation: Modifying a global variable "step" inside the local scope of function raises an UnboundLocalError.
learningTip: Declare global variables using the global keyword inside functions if they must be reassigned.

title: Threshold priority logic leak
explanation: Since 0.6 is greater than 0.2, it matches "Medium" and never reaches the "Hard" condition.
learningTip: When checking threshold limits, check strict values first (e.g. descending order for greater than checks).

title: Incorrect indent in def adjust
explanation: The function body is not indented under the def statement, causing an IndentationError.
learningTip: Ensure all function bodies are indented consistently.

title: No upper ceiling boundary logic leak
snippet:
# Adapt difficulty multiplier
mult = 1.0
def adapt(consecutive_correct):
    global mult
    mult = consecutive_correct * 0.5 # Expected: limit mult to max 3.0
explanation: Without an upper limit check, the multiplier can grow infinitely, causing unstable scale jumps.
learningTip: Always set boundary ceilings (using min/max or conditionals) for adaptive scaling variables.

title: Comparing complex values sorting key
explanation: By default, sorted(dict) only sorts dictionary keys alphabetically. Specify a custom key parameter.
learningTip: Use key parameter in sorted() to define customized sorting criteria.

title: Comparing instances of un-orderable classes
snippet:
# Compare objects
class User:
    def __init__(self, score):
        self.score = score
u1 = User(10)
u2 = User(20)
print(u1 < u2)
options: Both __lt__ implementation and direct score comparison work
correctAnswer: Both __lt__ implementation and direct score comparison work
explanation: Python 3 does not support default comparisons between custom class objects. You must define comparison methods like __lt__.
learningTip: Implement rich comparison magic methods (__lt__, __gt__, __eq__) to enable custom sorting of objects.

title: Truthy check matches False value
snippet:
# Check flag status
active = False
if active == True:
    print("Yes")
# Is checking explicit comparison matching boolean best practice?
options: No, simply use "if active:" or "if not active:"
correctAnswer: No, simply use "if active:" or "if not active:"
explanation: Explicit comparisons to True/False are redundant and unpythonic. Use "if active:" or "if not active:" directly.
learningTip: Test boolean variables directly without using explicit == True/False comparisons.

title: Missing double equal in function check
explanation: The return statement uses assignment (=) instead of equivalence comparison (==).
learningTip: Ensure you use == inside return conditions rather than =.

title: Invalid multi condition check logic
snippet:
# Check status
status = "active"
if status == "active" or "pending":
    print("OK")
explanation: The expression "status == 'active' or 'pending'" is evaluated as (status == 'active') or ('pending'). Since non-empty string 'pending' is always truthy, this condition is always True.
learningTip: Always write full comparison expressions for each check, or use membership checks (in).

title: Incorrect elif syntax after else
explanation: An "elif" block cannot appear after an "else" block. "else" must always be the final block in the statement.
learningTip: Always sequence blocks as: if -> elif -> else.

title: Logical AND checking bounds mistake
snippet:
# Check valid index
idx = 5
limit = 3
if idx >= 0 or idx < limit:
    print("Valid") # Expected: bounds check
explanation: Using "or" means only one condition must be true. Since 5 >= 0 is True, "Valid" is printed even though index 5 is out of bounds.
learningTip: Use logical "and" (or chained operators) to enforce range limits and index safety.

title: NameError variable defined only in one branch
snippet:
# Calculate discount
status = "guest"
if status == "vip":
    discount = 0.2
else:
    pass
final = 100 * (1 - discount)
explanation: If the else branch is taken, "discount" is never defined, causing a NameError when calculating final.
learningTip: Ensure all variables used after conditional blocks are defined in every potential branch.

title: Negated condition checking logic
snippet:
# Alert if mismatch
valid = False
if not valid == True:
    print("Mismatch") # Expected: print only if valid is False
options: The code logic is correct, but unpythonic
explanation: The expression "not valid == True" is valid but confusing. Use "if not valid:" or "if valid is False:" for clarity.
learningTip: Avoid double negatives or complex negations. Keep conditions direct.

title: Incorrect indentation level in nested condition
options: Align inner if with outer if
explanation: The statement print("Ten") must be indented under the inner "if x == 10:" block.
learningTip: Verify that indentation matches nested scopes accurately.

title: KeyError in nested dictionary access
explanation: Accessing a nested key that is missing raises a KeyError. Safely access using chained .get() methods with fallback dicts.
learningTip: Chaining dict.get(key, {}) permits safe lookup in deeply nested structures.

title: Dictionary update overwrites keys
options: The code behaves correctly, but check if keys overwrite
explanation: The code works. It updates opt1 with opt2 values. But to preserve original opt1, use a merge/copy syntax.
learningTip: Merging dictionaries can be done in-place with update(), or non-destructively using {**d1, **d2} or the | operator.

title: TypeError key is unhashable dict
explanation: Dictionaries are mutable and cannot be used as keys. Convert them to tuples, strings, or frozensets.
learningTip: Set and dictionary keys must consist of hashable, immutable data types.

title: Copy dict modification leak
snippet:
# Copy settings
orig = {"db": {"port": 3306}}
copy = orig.copy()
copy["db"]["port"] = 5432
print(orig["db"]["port"]) # Expected: 3306
options: Use deepcopy: import copy; copy_dict = copy.deepcopy(orig)
correctAnswer: Use deepcopy: import copy; copy_dict = copy.deepcopy(orig)
explanation: dict.copy() creates a shallow copy. Nested dictionary references remain shared. Use copy.deepcopy() to duplicate fully.
learningTip: Use deepcopy for complex nested data structures to avoid mutation leaks.

title: Incorrect dict comprehension dictionary key assignment
snippet:
# Create dict mapping square values
nums = [1, 2]
squares = [x: x*x for x in nums]
options: Use curly braces: {x: x*x for x in nums}
Use dict comprehension: dict((x, x*x) for x in nums)
explanation: Dict comprehensions require curly braces {} with key:value notation, not square brackets [].
learningTip: Use curly braces for dictionary comprehensions: {key: value for item in iterable}.

title: Mismatched f-string quotes
snippet:
# Welcome user
name = "Alice"
msg = f"User: {name}'
print(msg)
explanation: The string wrapper quotes must match (both double or both single).
learningTip: Ensure quotes encapsulating f-strings match exactly.

title: KeyError in format placeholders
snippet:
# Format name and status
msg = "User {name} is {status}".format(name="Alice")
explanation: The template string contains an explicit placeholder {status} that is not provided in format(), raising a KeyError.
learningTip: All named placeholders in string.format() must have corresponding keyword arguments.

title: Incorrect percentage float formatting
snippet:
# Print ratio as percentage
ratio = 0.756
print(f"{ratio:.2}") # Expected: 75.60%
explanation: "{ratio:.2}" only displays two significant digits (0.76). To format as percentage, use percentage notation ":.2%".
learningTip: Use formatting suffix "%" inside f-strings to automatically multiply by 100 and add a percent sign.

title: Incorrect f-string brackets nesting
explanation: This is valid in modern Python. Ensure quote marks inside square brackets differ from the outer f-string quotes.
learningTip: Vary double and single quotes between f-string wrappers and dictionary string keys.

title: ValueError incompatible format specifier
snippet:
# Format integer as float precision
val = "10"
print(f"{val:.2f}")
options: Convert val to float first: float(val)
explanation: Applying float formatting specs (:.2f) on a string variable raises a ValueError.
learningTip: Verify that the variables formatted with numeric specifiers are indeed floats or integers.

title: Non-default parameter follows default parameter
explanation: Parameters without default values must always be defined before parameters with default values.
learningTip: Place required arguments first, and optional arguments (with default values) last in function signatures.

title: Mutable default argument accumulator bug
snippet:
# Add user to session list
def add_user(u, users=[]):
    users.append(u)
    return users
print(add_user("Alice")) # ["Alice"]
print(add_user("Bob"))   # Expected: ["Bob"]
options:
def add_user(u, users=None):
    if users is None: users = []
    users.append(u)
    return users
correctAnswer:
def add_user(u, users=None):
    if users is None: users = []
    users.append(u)
    return users
explanation: Python evaluates default arguments once during function definition. Using a mutable list [] shares the same list instance across calls. Use None as default.
learningTip: Always use None as default value for mutable arguments, then initialize them inside the function body.

title: Missing return value causing None operations
snippet:
# Calculate double score
def double(s):
    res = s * 2
val = double(10) + 5
explanation: Functions without an explicit return statement return None by default. Adding 5 to None raises a TypeError.
learningTip: Make sure functions intended to compute a result explicitly call "return".

title: Incorrect global scope declaration inside parameter list
explanation: You cannot put global declarations inside a function's parameter definition signature list.
learningTip: Declare global scope configurations on their own line inside the function body if necessary.

title: Function name shadow overwriting built-in function
snippet:
# Find max score
def max(lst):
    return lst[0]
result = max([1, 2])
# Expected: Built-in max is still accessible
options: Use a different function name (e.g. find_max)
Built-in max is lost, rename custom function
explanation: Defining a custom function named "max" shadows the built-in max() function in the local namespace, making it inaccessible.
learningTip: Avoid naming your variables or functions after built-in Python functions (like sum, max, min, list).

title: IndexError popping from empty list index
snippet:
# Pop third index
vals = [1, 2]
val = vals.pop(3)
options: Both length check and try-except work
correctAnswer: Both length check and try-except work
explanation: Popping a non-existent index raises an IndexError.
learningTip: Check if length is greater than index before attempting pop(index).

title: In-place sort logic return overwrite
snippet:
# Sort grades
grades = [88, 92, 79]
grades = grades.sort(reverse=True)
print(grades) # Expected: [92, 88, 79]
explanation: Assigning grades to grades.sort() overwrites it with None. Sort in place or use sorted().
learningTip: List mutating operations return None. Never chain them with assignment.

title: AttributeError append list to list incorrectly
snippet:
# Add elements
nums1 = [1, 2]
nums2 = [3, 4]
nums1.append(nums2)
print(len(nums1)) # Expected: 4
explanation: append(nums2) inserts the list [3, 4] as a single nested element, making nums1 equal to [1, 2, [3, 4]]. Use extend() or +=.
learningTip: Use extend() to merge iterable items individually, and append() to insert an object as a single nested item.

title: Shallow list copy inner dictionary mutation leak
snippet:
# Duplicating list of dicts
orig = [{"score": 10}]
copy = list(orig)
copy[0]["score"] = 20
print(orig[0]["score"]) # Expected: 10
options: Use copy.deepcopy(): import copy; copy_list = copy.deepcopy(orig)
explanation: A shallow copy copies the list references. The inner dictionaries remain shared objects. Modifying the copy mutates the original.
learningTip: Use deepcopy to fully clone nested structures.

title: Incorrect slice syntax colons placement
snippet:
# Slice first two items
items = [10, 20, 30]
sub = items[0,,2]
options: Both sub = items[0:2] and sub = items[:2] work
correctAnswer: Both sub = items[0:2] and sub = items[:2] work
explanation: Python uses colons (:) to partition slices, not commas or double dots.
learningTip: Define slices using syntax: start:stop:step.

title: Modifying list elements in for loop without index
snippet:
# Double all scores
scores = [10, 20]
for s in scores:
    s *= 2
print(scores) # Expected: [20, 40]
options: Use range loop: for i in range(len(scores)): scores[i] *= 2
Use list comprehension: scores = [s * 2 for s in scores]
explanation: The variable "s" is a local reference inside the loop. Mutating it does not modify the list array cells. Use index loop or comprehension.
learningTip: To modify list elements in place, loop using indices or use a list comprehension.

title: Mutation during dict iteration
options: Iterate over list: for k in list(config):
explanation: Modifying dictionary keys size directly during iteration throws a RuntimeError.
learningTip: Convert keys to a static list before iterating and mutating dictionary keys.

title: Passing mutable argument to modify values
snippet:
# Add tag
def tag(lst):
    lst.append("new")
my_list = [1]
tag(my_list)
# Is my_list mutated outside the function?
options: Yes, lists are mutable and passed by reference
Yes, but copy must be returned
correctAnswer: Yes, lists are mutable and passed by reference
explanation: This behaves as expected (lst is mutated). If you want to prevent mutation, pass a copy of the list (like my_list.copy()).
learningTip: Be aware that passing mutable objects to functions allows in-place mutations outside the scope.

title: Incorrect set add mutation syntax
explanation: The += operator is not supported for set values. Use add() or update().
learningTip: Mutate sets using add() or update() methods.

title: Unintended object attribute mutation shared state
snippet:
# Shared class attribute mutation leak
class Session:
    users = [] # Shared class attribute

s1 = Session()
s2 = Session()
s1.users.append("Alice")
print(s2.users) # Expected: []
explanation: Declaring users directly in the class scope makes it a class attribute shared across all instances. Initialize it in __init__ as an instance variable.
learningTip: Declare instance attributes inside the __init__ constructor to isolate state between object instances.

title: Linear search early return bug
options: Move return False out of the loop body
explanation: The return False is inside the loop block, executing on the first mismatch. Indent it outside the loop.
learningTip: Verify loop indentation. Returns for failures should execute only after loop completes.

title: AttributeError index lookup on dictionary
options: Find key: idx = [k for k, v in mapping.items() if v == 20][0]
correctAnswer: Find key: idx = [k for k, v in mapping.items() if v == 20][0]
explanation: Dictionaries do not have an index() method. Search through items() using loop or comprehension.
learningTip: To search values in a dictionary, iterate through items() and compare values to keys.

title: Substring check index edge case
snippet:
# Search substring prefix
text = "ERROR: connection failed"
if text.find("ERROR"):
    print("Alert") # Expected: print Alert
explanation: text.find("ERROR") returns 0 (the index). Since 0 is falsy, the condition fails. Use "in" or compare with != -1.
learningTip: Use membership checking (in) or explicit index boundaries checks when using find().

title: Incorrect search list filter lambda syntax
snippet:
# Filter items
items = [1, 2, 3]
result = filter(items, lambda x: x > 1)
options: result = [x for x in items if x > 1]
explanation: The arguments in filter() are (function, iterable). The code lists items first. Also, list comprehensions are preferred.
learningTip: Pass function first, then iterable to filter(), or use list comprehensions.

title: Binary search infinite loop condition
snippet:
# Binary search step
def search(lst, target):
    low, high = 0, len(lst) - 1
    while low <= high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        if lst[mid] == target: return mid
        elif lst[mid] < target: low = mid # Expected: low = mid + 1
explanation: Setting low = mid instead of mid + 1 causes an infinite loop when low and high differ by 1, as mid does not progress.
learningTip: Ensure indices are strictly adjusted (+1 or -1) in binary search loops to prevent hang states.

title: AttributeError splitlines on integer
snippet:
# Format lines
code = 123
lines = code.splitlines()
explanation: splitlines() is a string method. Calling it on an integer raises an AttributeError.
learningTip: Cast numeric variables to string before executing text parsing methods.

title: String split empty delimiter logic leak
snippet:
# Split words
text = "a  b"
words = text.split(" ") # Expected: ["a", "b"]
options: words = [w for w in text.split(" ") if w]
explanation: Splitting with an explicit space delimiter " " preserves empty strings between double spaces, returning ["a", "", "b"]. Call split() without arguments to group all whitespaces.
learningTip: Use string.split() with no arguments to automatically clean and split arbitrary spaces.

title: IndexError slice out of range string index
snippet:
# Safe string index slicing
text = "abc"
print(text[5]) # Expected: empty string if out of range
explanation: Accessing a single index out of range text[5] raises an IndexError, but slicing text[5:6] returns empty string "" safely.
learningTip: Slicing in Python does not raise IndexErrors for out-of-bounds indices.

title: String encoding check failure
snippet:
# Check character encoding
word = "cafe"
# Convert to bytes
data = word.encode()
# Expected: data to compare as string
if data == "cafe": print("match")
explanation: Encoding a string returns a bytes object. Comparing bytes directly with a string literal returns False.
learningTip: Prefix bytes literals with b (e.g. b"text") or decode bytes to strings before comparing.

title: Incorrect string format formatting placeholders brackets
snippet:
# Format name
name = "Alice"
msg = "Hello [name]".format(name=name)
explanation: String format template variables must use curly braces {}, not square brackets [].
learningTip: Use {} braces inside template strings when using the .format() method.

title: ValueError unhandled cast failure
options: Use try-except: try: return int(text) except ValueError: return None
explanation: Casting non-numeric strings using int() raises a ValueError. Wrap in a try-except block or check .isdigit().
learningTip: Always parse and validate user input inside try-except blocks when converting types.

title: Validation logic leak empty input
options: if not name or len(name) > 10: return False
if len(name) == 0 or len(name) > 10: return False
explanation: The code returns True for an empty name "", which is typically invalid.
learningTip: Always validate against empty strings or None values first.

title: KeyError in configuration check
explanation: Accessing missing key "port" raises a KeyError. Use .get() or check membership first.
learningTip: Validate dictionary keys safely using dict.get(key) with fallback defaults.

title: Incorrect validate function parameter format signature
explanation: While Python allows a trailing comma in argument lists, check if parameter syntax is clean without dangling commas.
learningTip: Keep function parameter lists clean and standard.

title: Validation regex email match checks
snippet:
# Validate email domain
email = "user@gmail.com"
if email.endswith("gmail"): print("valid") # Expected: check full domain
explanation: Using endswith("gmail") returns False because it ends with ".com". Check full domain suffix.
learningTip: Use explicit suffix patterns or splits to validate domain names exactly.

title: Infinite loop condition never updates
snippet:
# Decrease countdown
i = 5
while i > 0:
    print(i)
    # Expected: loop ends at 0
explanation: The counter variable "i" is never decremented inside the loop, creating an infinite loop.
learningTip: Make sure the variables involved in a while loop condition are updated in each iteration.

title: IndexError list pop while loop condition
snippet:
# Process items
items = [1, 2]
while len(items) >= 0:
    val = items.pop()
explanation: The condition "len(items) >= 0" is always True. When the list is empty, popping from it raises an IndexError.
learningTip: Use "while items:" as a clean way to process list items until the collection is empty.

title: While loop condition off by one boundary
snippet:
# Add numbers until total is 10
total = 0
while total < 10:
    total += 3
# Expected total to not exceed 10. Wait, if total exceeds 10, loop terminates. Let's check another one.
explanation: The loop runs, but the final total is 12 (exceeds 10). Add checks inside the loop or adjust addition.
learningTip: Ensure loop exit conditions and value additions are bounded correctly.

title: Incorrect while colon placement
snippet:
# Simple while
i = 0
while i < 3
    print(i)
    i += 1
options: Add do keyword: while i < 3 do:
explanation: Python while loops require a colon at the end of the conditional header line.
learningTip: Always append a colon to while headers.

title: While True break condition missing
snippet:
# Loop input
while True:
    user_input = "exit"
    # Expected: exit loop if user_input is "exit"
explanation: Without an explicit "break" statement inside a "while True" loop, the loop runs infinitely.
learningTip: Always provide a path to hit a "break" statement inside while True loops.
